title: Late to Recessions: Stocks and the Business Cycle
abstract: I find that returns are predictably negative for several months after the onset of recessions, becoming high only thereafter. I identify business cycle turning points by estimating a state-space model using macroeconomic data. Conditioning on the business cycle further reveals that returns exhibit momentum in recessions, whereas in expansions they display the mild reversals expected from discount rate changes. A strategy exploiting this pattern produces positive alphas. Using analyst forecast data, I show that my findings are consistent with investors' slow reaction to recessions. When expected returns are negative, analysts are too optimistic and their downward expectation revisions are exceptionally high.

article.introduction:
A large and expanding literatureexplores the relation between stock returns and the business cycle. The traditional view is that stocks are rationally priced to immediately reflect investors' expectations about future economic activity and that expected excess returns on stocks are positive, vary over time, and display countercyclical behavior.1I argue that stock prices are less efficient at incorporating business cycle fluctuations than what the literature has previously acknowledged. Specifically, I show that stock prices reflect information signaling the onset of recessions with an economically significant delay, which has important consequences for the behavior of expected returns.2
I show that the state of the business cycle is far more informative about expected stock returns than previously recognized. I identify business cycle turning points by estimating a state-space model using real-time macroeconomic data. The central finding is that returns are predictably negative for several months following the onset of recessions before rising to persistent higher-than-average levels. Further conditioning on the business cycle reveals a strong state-dependence in autocorrelations. Returns exhibit substantial momentum in recessions, whereas in expansions they display the mild reversals expected from discount rate changes. I propose a model of expected and realized returns that optimally accounts for these business cycle patterns. Using the model's expected return estimate, I then construct a trading strategy to analyze the implied return predictability and assess my findings' economic relevance. The strategy produces significant risk-adjusted returns: for the aggregate market, it earns an annualized alpha of 5.4%, increases Sharpe ratios by 30% over the fully invested buy-and-hold approach, and produces a significant monthly out-of-sampleR2$R^2$statistic of 1.7%.
The right panel of Figure1depicts the central finding. An investor who buys stocks in the first four months of a recession, as defined by the recession probability (see the left panel of Figure1), and holds them for six months earns an annualized average excess return of −8%.3However, these negative returns are reversed in the following 12 months with persistently high excess market returns of around 13%. After a year, returns converge to the unconditional equity premium, depicted by the horizontal bar in the right panel of Figure1. From a market participant perspective, this pattern suggests that the investor can profit by selling at the start of recessions and then increasing risk-taking several months later. From a risk-based perspective, the empirical pattern in Figure1presents a puzzle because it is hard to explain negative average excess returns within any rational equilibrium model. Deepening the puzzle is the fact that these negative returns arise in bad macroeconomic times, precisely when risk-based theories generally suggest that compensation for risk should be positive and high. As I argue below, the pattern instead appears more consistent with the idea that investors are late to act on macroeconomic news that signals the start of recessions.
My proposed econometric model has two main ingredients. The first is a regime-switching model for expected and realized returns, where the regimes are linked to business cycle phases. I treat conditional expected returns as a latent variable that follows a first-order autoregressive process. Importantly, I allow discount rate volatility to vary across the business cycle and I allow shocks between the realized return and expected returns to be correlated. The second ingredient identifies the economic regimes via an econometric model that uses macro variables to extract a diffusion index and a real-time recession probability forecast as in Hamilton (1989), Kim and Nelson (1998), and Chauvet (1998).
To account for both model ingredients jointly, I draw from the growing literature on density forecast combination.4Specifically, the model dynamically combines the predictive densities from two individual models that differ in the amount by which expected returns vary using time-varying weights. These dynamic model weights correspond to the probability of being in an expansion or a recession. My setup takes the form of a high-dimensional Markov-switching state-space model tailored toward monthly real-time data. A mixed-frequency approach and temporal aggregation allow me to sharpen inference about the regimes by incorporating the information contained in quarterly time-aggregated data and at the same time address variable reporting lags, early announcements, and missing observations of each particular macro series. Importantly, to avoid any look-ahead bias, I restrict the information set to those observations that were known and available to investors at each point in time and I only use data up to timet$t$to forecastt+1$t+1$returns.
The model utilizes the state-dependent serial correlation in returns to predict future returns. When expected returns are latent, realized lagged returns are noisy signals about discount rates. The model's estimation reveals that discount rates are persistent, fairly stable in expansions, and much more volatile during recessions. Moreover, return surprises and innovations in expected returns are strongly negatively correlated, consistent with Campbell (1991), Campbell and Ammer (1993), and Pástor and Stambaugh (2009). All other things being equal, the persistence and variability of expected returns generate a positive autocorrelation in returns and thus prior returns positively predict future returns. However, this positive effect is overwhelmed in expansions by the countervailing effect of return innovations that are highly negatively correlated with shocks to expected returns. In this state, returns exhibit mild negative autocorrelation, and thus low past returns predict high future returns. When expected returns are much more volatile in recessions, the positive effect dominates so that returns are positively autocorrelated, and hence the model updates forecasts of future returns in the same direction as recent realized returns. Notably, the weights used on lagged returns to compute the expected return measure are derived from optimal filtering techniques.
Using the model's estimate of expected returns, I analyze my findings' economic relevance via a trading strategy. Specifically, I treat the measure of expected returns as the conditional beta in a standard conditional capital asset pricing model (CAPM) framework and increase (decrease) risk-taking when the expected return estimate is high (low). In line with Figure1, the strategy generates a positive unconditional CAPM alpha by reducing risk-taking for several months following the onset of a recession. At such times, the estimates of expected returns are negative—not positive and high. Shortly after completely cashing out, the strategy returns to the market and increases risk-taking substantially. Put differently, the strategy produces profits in part by avoiding stock market crashes associated with recessions. The rest of the profits come from expansionary periods. During expansions, the strategy takes a contrarian position by exploiting mild mean reversion in returns . I show that although mean reversion is small in the data, it is crucial in a cumulative sense for forecasting future monthly returns. The strategy's substantial gains may be surprising given the prevailing consensus in the literature that stock return predictability is highly unstable (e.g., Ferson, Sarkissian, and Simin (2003a), Goyal and Welch (2008)) and concentrated in short-lived predictability pockets (e.g., Timmermann (2008), Farmer, Schmidt, and Timmermann (2019)).
To evaluate the robustness of the strategy, I conduct a wide range of tests. The strategy overlaps with the time-series momentum factor of Moskowitz, Ooi, and Pedersen (2012), since, during recessions, the model of expected returns updates the forecasts of future returns in the same direction as recent realized returns. The strategy also coincides with the volatility-managed market factor of Moreira and Muir (2017), since the strategy also reduces risk-taking during bad times. I show that my strategy is different from these two factors and produces positive alpha when controlling for them. Specifically, I document that accounting for mild mean reversion in returns during expansions is important for return predictability. Moreover, one principal difference between the volatility-managed factor and my strategy relates to increased risk-taking during recessions. The volatility-managed factor will gradually return to the market only when volatility decreases and not necessarily when the market rebounds.
I extend the empirical analysis of my strategy in theInternet Appendix.5I first look at the cross section of U.S. state portfolios and regional business cycles and show that the results continue to hold. I further show that the strategy can be improved by also timing volatility as in Moreira and Muir (2017). Moreover, the strategy produces fairly large benefits even when subject to tight constraints on leverage and short selling and to realistic transaction costs. The strategy generates similar alphas across different subsample periods and for a wide range of pricing factors. I also verify that the strategy is profitable using utility-based performance measures, the manipulation-proof performance measures of Goetzmann et al. (2007), or the regression-based timing measures of Treynor and Mazuy (1966) and Henriksson and Merton (1981). Notably, the strategy's outperformance vanishes if I turn off the state-dependent autocorrelation in returns. Given the strategy's profitability and robustness, I conclude that the model provides a good measure of the market expected returns over the monthly horizon.
I analyze these findings' economic implications in terms of the behavior of expected returns and examine the type of models we need to match these facts. I first explore standard models with rational expectations and countercyclical risk premia. Earning a positive unconditional alpha by reducing risk-taking during bad times is difficult to explain in terms of risk. In the context of these models, the alpha would be either negative or close to zero. As shown by Lewellen and Nagel (2006), if the conditional CAPM holds and the market volatility is constant, the unconditional alpha depends primarily on how the conditional risk exposure,βt$\beta _t$, covaries with the unobservable market risk premium,γt$\gamma _t$, that is,α≈cov(βt,γt)$\alpha \approx \operatorname{cov}(\beta _{t}, \gamma _{t})$. Hence, a strategy that avoids taking risks during a recession's early stages will tend to earn a negative alpha because the market risk premium is high precisely during these times. If the risk premium and volatility move together, as implied by many asset pricing theories, the volatility will tend to offset the impact of the risk premium and shrink the negativeα$\alpha$toward zero.
Instead, the evidence appears to be more consistent with an initial underreaction to news, that is, news that signals the start of a recession is incorporated only slowly into prices, which induces the observed abnormal price drift during these times.6To better understand this explanation, I explore a model in which investors use the present-value identity to value stocks but discount future cash flows using subjective expectations. If subjective beliefs about future cash flows react to new information with a lag, then expected returns are a function of a rational risk premia component and near-term forecastable changes in cash flows that are not yet incorporated into prices. Hence, overestimating future cash flows at the onset of the recession under the subjective measure offsets the positive increase in risk premia and imputes negative return predictability for the econometrician. Consistent with this sluggish subjective belief updating, I find that downward revisions in analyst expectations about cash flows are exceptionally high, and forecasts are too optimistic when my measure of expected returns is negative. These results are in line with recent papers that emphasize underreaction to aggregate news at short horizons. Coibion and Gorodnichenko (2015) find evidence of information stickiness in macro-forecasts from professional forecasters, while Bouchaud et al. (2019) document economically significant expectation stickiness at the firm level from financial analysts' forecasts about future cash flows. Moreover, Katz, Lustig, and Nielsen (2017) show that local stock markets adjust sluggishly to changes in local inflation.
During expansions, I cannot determine whether the strategy's profits are due to rational variation in expected returns or to behavioral biases. Mean reversion in returns is consistent with rational discount rate movements as explained in Cochrane (2005). Alternatively, mild negative autocorrelation in returns could also reflect belief overreaction at longer horizons consistent with recent papers studying biases in beliefs (e.g., Angeletos, Huo, and Sastry (2021), Bordalo et al. (2020), De La O and Myers (2021) and Kohlhas and Walther (2021)). See also Angeletos, Huo, and Sastry (2021) for how a simple model with dispersed, noisy information and overextrapolation can explain initial underreaction and delayed overreaction of beliefs in response to shocks.
The paper is organized as follows. SectionIpresents novel stylized facts on business cycle variations in stock returns. SectionIIintroduces the econometric framework. SectionIIIpresents estimation results. SectionIVmeasures the economic relevance of my findings. SectionVanalyzes the time-series behavior of my expected return estimates. SectionVIestablishes and rules out potential alternative explanations for my findings. SectionVIIconcludes. A detailedInternet Appendixextends my empirical analysis along many fronts.
article.I. Stylized Facts:
This section presents the paper's novel stylized facts. Many market downturns and upturns happen during recessions, but the central insight from Figure1is that these directional changes do not occur randomly. At the onset of economic contractions, one is more likely to observe market downturns, which are then followed by upturns. I present this stylized fact by introducing a feasible and straightforward strategy that trades on this pattern. The second stylized fact is a strong state-dependent autocorrelation in returns. I document this via simple ordinary least squares (OLS) regressions. SectionIIpresents a model of expected and realized returns that optimally accounts for these business cycle patterns.
The trading strategy switches from stocks (e.g., S&P 500) to cash (e.g., one-month T-bill return) during bad economic times and market downturns, while it stays fully invested in stocks in the remaining periods. Specifically, suppose at the end of the month that the U.S. industrial production growth (year-over-year) series and the realized market return are both below their 12-month moving average, indicating a contractionary period and a market downturn, respectively. In this case, the strategy goes 100% into cash; otherwise, the strategy goes 100% into stocks. The series for the market return and the risk-free rate come from Kenneth R. French's website.7TheInternet Appendixdiscusses robustness with respect to both the choice of the 12-month moving average and the sample period.
Column (1) of Panel A of TableIreports performance measures for this simple trading strategy. From January 1980 to December 2019, the strategy earned an annualized mean excess return of 7.24% with an annualized standard deviation of 12.64% by being fully invested in stocks 77% of the time. For comparison, a fixed-allocation strategy that is invested in stocks the same unconditional amount of time, that is, a mix of 77% in equities and 23% in T-bills, generates an annualized mean excess return of 6.33% and volatility of 11.82%, as shown in column (4). These numbers imply that the simple strategy produces an overall Sharpe ratio increase of 6.92% over the 77/23 strategy. Moreover, the simple strategy generates an annualized CAPM alpha of 1.63% (t-statistic = 1.67). Panel B of TableIreports average maximum drawdowns in the National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) expansions and recessions to help us understand in which phase of the business cycle the strategy loses money relative to the fixed-allocation portfolio. The strategy benefits by reducing the maximum drawdowns during recessions. For instance, the average maximum drawdown during recessions is 16.49%, which is significantly lower than the corresponding 18.96% for the 77/23 strategy.
Figure2shows the cumulative monthly returns for investments in the market portfolio, the risk-free asset, the 77/23 portfolio, the main strategy that conditions on the growth and return signals, and a strategy that conditions only on the growth signal. All strategies invest $1 in July 1927 and close the position in December 2019. The gains of the main strategy (assuming no transaction costs) would total around $10,000 at the end of 2019 (see the dark blue line) versus about $2,000 for the fixed-allocation 77/23 strategy (dotted black line) and $6,000 for the buy-and-hold strategy (dotted gray line). The figure confirms the central message of TableI: the strategy outperforms its benchmark by reducing risk-taking in low economic growth periods during market downturns. This result does not contradict the long empirical evidence on countercyclical expected returns (e.g., Lettau and Ludvigson (2010), Lustig and Verdelhan (2012)). For instance, a strategy that goes into cash during the entire duration of the contractionary period significantly underperforms by generating around $443 at the end of the sample (see the dotted light blue line). However, as I argue below, there is a mismatch between macro risk and compensation for risk in the first months of contractionary periods. These months are particularly bad times to invest in stocks, and an active strategy would ideally cash out during these times and buy back in after the downward pressure on prices dissipates. This is the first stylized fact.
I document two additional results. First, I can increase the strategy's profit by improving the measure of economic activity. To do so, I estimate the growth signal from various macroeconomic indicators rather than just using the industrial production growth series. This result makes sense because business cycle fluctuations are about the comovement of a broad range of economic indicators. Column (2) of TableIshows results for a strategy that uses the common growth component estimated out of sample from nine real-time variables that capture different aspects of overall U.S. macroeconomic activity. I introduce and estimate this variable in SectionII. Specifically, a bad economic time occurs when the common growth component is below its 12-month moving average. Under this broader measure of economic activity, the strategy is fully invested in stocks 75% of the time and increases Sharpe ratios by around 25% over its 75/25 fixed-allocation benchmark. Moreover, it increases the annualized CAPM alpha to 3.26% by reducing the average maximum drawdowns during recessions to 14.02%.
As shown in columns (1) through (3) of TableII, conditioning on the state of the business cycle reveals that during bad economic times, returns are strongly positively autocorrelated. For instance, when the industrial production growth series is below its 12-month moving average, the point estimate is 0.11 (=b̂0+b̂Ind.Prod$=\hat{b}_0 +\hat{b}_{\text{Ind.Prod}}$) with at-statistic of 1.92 on the difference. Notably, the point estimate increases to 0.36 (=b̂0+b̂NBER$= \hat{b}_0 + \hat{b}_{\text{NBER}}$) when I use the ex post NBER recessionary index.8These statistically significant positive coefficients during bad times are evidence of price momentum in the aggregate market and imply that a string of past negative (positive) returns forecast future negative (positive) returns. Conversely, the point estimates for the autocorrelation coefficient in good economic times (equal tob̂0$\hat{b}_0$) are negative but not statistically significant. This mild negative autocorrelation in returns means that past bad returns forecast future good returns. In the next sections, I show that although the point estimateb̂0$\hat{b}_0$is small and statistically indistinguishable from zero, exploiting mild reversals in returns during expansions is important in a cumulative sense for forecasting future returns. Finally, as shown in column (4), without conditioning on the business cycle, we would have missed this sign-flipping pattern in the autocorrelation of returns.
article.II. Econometric Framework:
The econometric model has two main ingredients. The first, described in SectionII.A, is a regime-switching model for realized stock returns, where I treat expected returns as a latent variable that follows a first-order autoregressive process. The model allows for both much more volatile expected return fluctuations during recessions and a negative correlation between return surprises and innovation in expected returns. As shown in SectionII.B, these two features allow the model to optimally account for the business cycle dependence in the autocorrelation of returns and to determine how the optimal filtering technique uses the noisy signals in lagged returns to form an expected returns estimate. The second ingredient, described in SectionII.C, is an econometric model that extracts a diffusion index from a large cross section of mixed-frequency macro variables to identify the economic regimes. In SectionII.D, I model both ingredients jointly via a dynamic linear prediction pool and describe the estimation procedure.
When expected returns are not observable, the econometrician's estimate for the equity premium implied by the model of returns described above is a weighted average of past realized excess returns and the probability of being in each economic regime.
To improve clarity of the discussion that follows, throughout this section I use the parameter estimates in TableIVobtained from the in-sample estimation of the model, that is, using all data available from January 1965 to December 2019. I describe the estimation procedure and the data set used in SectionIII.
Depending on the parameter values, the model-implied autocorrelation can be positive or negative. The negative component of (7) arises if unexpected returns and shocks to expected returns are negatively correlated (i.e.,ρμ,r<0$ \rho _{\mu ,r}<0$). Empirically, I find that this is the case. The posterior median estimate forρμ,r$ \rho _{\mu ,r}$is equal to −0.95, which is consistent with implied estimates in the literature (e.g., Campbell (1991), Campbell and Ammer (1993)). An intuitive way to understand this negative correlation is by imposing the present-value relation to decompose unexpected returns,εt+1r$\varepsilon ^{r}_{{t+1}}$, into revisions in expectations about the current and future dividend growth,CFNt+1$CFN_{t+1}$, and revisions in expected future returns,DRNt+1$DRN_{t+1}$, (i.e.,εt+1r=CFNt+1−DRNt+1$\varepsilon ^{r}_{{t+1}} = CFN_{t+1} - DRN_{t+1}$). Broadly, suppose expected dividend growth rates are not too volatile. Then, unexpected returns are driven primarily by expected return shocks and the correlation between these two shocks will be negative and close to minus one. Pástor and Stambaugh (2009) provide conditions forCFNt+1$CFN_{t+1}$andDRNt+1$ DRN_{t+1}$under whichρμ,r<0$ \rho _{\mu ,r}<0$.10
The positive component of (7) comes from the persistence and variability of expected returns. As shown in TableIV, the posterior median ofρ$\rho$is 0.97, pointing toward reasonably persistent expected excess returns, consistent with Fama and French (1988), Ferson, Sarkissian, and Simin (2003b), Pástor and Stambaugh (2009), and van Binsbergen and Koijen (2010). Moreover, the fraction of variance in excess returns explained byμt$\mu _t$, as measured byϕ(St)$\phi (S_{t})$, is state-dependent and higher during recessions. The posterior median value forϕ(St=Exp.)$\phi (S_t=\text{Exp.})$during expansions is around 0.92%, suggesting that expected returns are fairly stable during these times. In contrast, expected returns are much more volatile during recessions, withϕ(St=Rec.)$\phi (S_t=\text{Rec.})$values of around 14%. This business cycle variation inϕ(St)$\phi (S_t)$could be consistent with a variety of leading asset pricing models in which variation in expected returns is usually linked to movements in state variables linked to the economy (e.g., Campbell and Cochrane (1999), Bansal and Yaron (2004), Gabaix (2012)). These state variables generally vary over time in a persistent fashion, giving rise to persistent expected returns. Moreover, forϕ(St)$\phi (S_t)$to vary across economic regimes, we would need these state variables that determine risk premia to fluctuate differently across economic regimes and be more volatile during recessions.11
The central finding is that the relative strength of these two offsetting components in equation (7) varies with the business cycle. In expansions, the positive component coming from persistent and mildly variable expected returns is overwhelmed by the countervailing effect of return innovations that are highly negatively correlated with shocks to expected returns. In this state, a positive return shock induces a long string of mild negative returns that cumulatively bring prices back toward their original price. As shown in Panel A of TableIII, this generates a small negative return autocorrelation, that is, we have mean reversion in returns.12Conversely, in recessions, when expected returns are much more volatile, the positive component of (7) dominates, generating a strong positive return autocorrelation. Therefore, in recessions, a negative shock to returns sets off a string of negative returns, which add momentum to the original price decrease. Notably, as shown in Panel A of TableIII, the widths of the 90% credible intervals for the model-implied autocorrelations are reasonably tight, highlighting that the model imposes enough econometric structure to sharpen inference about the state-dependent autocorrelation in returns.
The OLS regression coefficients in TableIIsuggest a strikingly high amount of autocorrelation conditional on low economic growth periods and mild negative autocorrelation in good economic times. For instance, when the common growth series is below its 12-month moving average, the point estimate is 0.09 (=b̂0+b̂Common.Growth$=\hat{b}_0 +\hat{b}_{\text{Common.Growth}}$) with at-statistic of 1.94 on the difference. Alternatively, when the series is above its moving average, the point estimate equals −0.01 (=b̂0$=\hat{b}_0$). TheInternet Appendixshows that such high and low values are statistically indistinguishable from the model-implied regression coefficients based on simulations from its posterior distribution.13Significantly, as I show next, the estimate of expected returns improves upon the OLS regression in equation (1) because it not only uses the previously realized return but also considers the entire history of lagged returns.
When expected returns are not observable, realized returns are noisy signals about the past (persistent) expected returns. To show how lagged excess returns are informative about future excess returns, I consider the case in which the economy stays in one economic regime throughout. When I condition on the economic regimeSt$S_{t}$, the model is linear. Hence, I can use Kalman-filtering steps to track the Gaussian distribution of the equity premiumμtj$\mu ^{j}_t$, where thej$j$superscript indicates that I am conditioning on statej$j$.
Throughout this section, to build intuition into the model mechanism, I considered the case in which the economy remained in one single regime. However, the filtered measure of expected returns,μ̂t+1|t$\hat{\mu }_{t+1|t}$, in equation (6) dynamically combines these predictive densities, which differ in the way they aggregate the information contained in lagged returns using time-varying weights. These dynamic model weights correspond to the probability of being in an expansion or recession. From an econometric perspective, the measureμ̂t+1|t$\hat{\mu }_{t+1|t}$accommodates parameter instability (e.g., Paye and Timmermann (2006), Rapach and Wohar (2006), Spiegel (2008)) and allows for large breaks in the model coefficients by tying them to business cycle turning points.
In the benchmark specification model of returns described in equations (2), (3), and (4), I make two main assumptions. First, I allow expected returns shocks and unexpected returns to be correlated as measured by the parameterρμ,r$\rho _{\mu ,r}$. Second, I allow the fraction of variance in excess returns explained byμt$\mu _t$to take different values in recessions and expansions, as measured byϕ(St=Rec.)$\phi (S_t = Rec.)$andϕ(St=Exp.)$\phi (S_t = Exp.)$, respectively. In the sections that follow, I show that these two ingredients generate a good model of expected returns.
In the benchmark model, I also assume thatρμ,r$\rho _{\mu ,r}$and the persistence of expected returns,ρ$\rho$, are stable across regimes. But it could well be the case thatρμ,r$\rho _{\mu ,r}$andρ$\rho$are state-dependent and vary with the business cycle.17Moreover, as shown in equation (7), ifρ$\rho$andρμ,r$\rho _{\mu ,r}$increase during recessions and decrease during expansions, then these time-varying parameters could account for the state-dependent autocorrelation in returns. TheInternet Appendixexplores these alternative specifications. I find thatρμ,r$\rho _{\mu ,r}$is stable across regimes. That is, I cannot reject the null hypothesis thatρμ,r$\rho _{\mu ,r}$takes different values across the business cycle. With respect to the persistence of expected returns, I find thatρ$\rho$is slightly lower during recessions. Based on predictive checks, I find that this specification generates an autocorrelation in returns that is too low relative to the data moments shown in TableII. Finally, I perform a formal specification test and find that the benchmark model of returns is preferred over models in whichρ$\rho$andρμ,r$\rho _{\mu ,r}$depend on the stateSt$S_t$.
Overall, the combination of data available at different frequencies, where each series starts at a different period, temporal aggregation, and accounting for missing observations because of the real-time nature of the data set in conjunction with the regime changes leads to a high-dimensional state-space model.
article.III. Data and Estimation Results:
When computing the measure of expected returns in equation (6), it is critical to avoid any look-ahead biases arising from both the data set used and the estimation procedure. I address this concern in two ways. First, I restrict the information set to those observations that were available to investors at each point in time. Second, I estimate the model out of sample in SectionIV.
I build a large data set of real-time variables. Real-time data simulate the actual information set available to investors and account for the fact that macro variables are sometimes released with a delay or receive substantial revisions by the statistical agencies following publication.20
I employ nine real-time macroeconomic series that capture different parts of the economy and are available at a monthly or quarterly frequency. Specifically, I consider variables related to real output and income (e.g., industrial production, gross domestic product), employment and hours (e.g., initial jobless claims), consumer spending (e.g., consumption expenditure), investment (e.g., private residential/nonresidential fixed investment), and inflation (e.g., the Consumer Price Index). All of the series start in different periods, but all end in December 2019. The macro and financial indicators are the data counterpart ofyi,t$y_{i,t}$from SectionII.C, and I provide a detailed description of each in theInternet Appendix. Data for the excess market return,re$r^{e}$, comes from Kenneth R. French's website.
TableIVprovides percentiles of the prior and posterior distributions. I obtain these estimates in the in-sample estimation of the model using data between January 1965 and December 2019. To facilitate interpretation of the model parameters, I reexpress the parameters in the covariance matrix,Σ(St)$\Sigma (S_t)$, in (4) in terms of the conditionalR2$R^2$,ϕ(St)$\phi (S_{t})$, in equation (8) and the conditional variance of realized excess returns,var(rte|St)$\text{var}(r^{e}_{t} | S_t)$. The priors are fairly agnostic. For instance, the prior forρμ,r$\rho _{\mu ,r}$, the parameter that determines the correlation between unexpected returns and shocks to expected returns, is a uniform distribution on the interval [−1, 0]. For the parametersϕ(St=Exp.)$\phi (S_t=Exp.)$andϕ(St=Rec.)$\phi (S_t= Rec.)$, I use priors that are uniform on the interval [0, 0.2], thereby allowing expected returns to be equally volatile in recessionary and expansionary regimes. Concerning the posterior distribution, the most important findings are that (i) discount rates are persistent, (ii) return surprises and expected return news are strongly negatively correlated, and (iii) discount rates are relatively stable in expansions and much more volatile during recessions.
Figure4plots the filtered one-month-ahead excess return forecast,μ̂t+1|t$\hat{\mu }_{t+1|t}$, as well as the mean excess return. The light gray bars indicate NBER recession dates. The figure shows that the filtered expected return series fluctuates around its unconditional mean. During recessions, the model updates forecasts of future excess returns in the same direction as recent realized returns. Because the market has usually recently crashed, the estimate of expected returns is negative. It remains low for several months following the onset of recessions before rising to higher-than-average levels once the economy starts to recover. Notably, negative expected returns are fairly rare and are concentrated in bad economic times. This pattern is the paper's most striking finding. In expansions, the usual Campbell-Shiller logic prevails, and recent realized positive returns are associated with negative updates to expected returns. Prominently, the sequence of positive realized returns during the dot-com bubble in the late 1990s decreased the filtered expected return to values almost statistically indistinguishable from zero. Thus, the reason for the higher-than-expected realized returns during that period is that expected returns fell, leading to an increase in prices that ultimately generated positive returns.
article.IV. Economic Relevance:
I perform a series of tests onrt+1e,μ$r^{e,\mu }_{t+1}$andμ̂t+1|t$\hat{\mu }_{t+1|t}$to establish the economic relevance of my findings. Importantly, for all of the results that follow, I estimateμ̂t+1|t$\hat{\mu }_{t+1|t}$on recursively increasing samples of real-time data. That is, at any given point in time,t$t$, I only use data up to datet$t$to estimateμ̂t+1|t$\hat{\mu }_{t+1|t}$as well as the economic regimeSt$S_t$. Moreover, as described in SectionIII, I only use those observations that were publicly available at any pointt$t$. I start my out-of-sample computations in January 1980. Using data between January 1965 and December 1979 to estimate the model, I compute the expected return for January 1980. I then use data between January 1965 and January 1980 to estimate the model and compute the forecast for February 1980. I proceed recursively in this way up until December 2019.
Column (1) of TableVreports results for the main specification. Panel A shows that the estimated marketα$\alpha$is both economically large (5.46% annualized) and statistically significant (t-statistic = 2.77), thereby suggesting thatμ̂t+1|t$\hat{\mu }_{t+1|t}$is positively related to the unobservable market's conditional expected return.
To further quantify the economic relevance of my results, the left panel of Figure5depicts the cumulative excess return of the main strategy from 1980 to 2019. As the figure shows, the strategy (dark blue line) generates a relatively steady stream of positive returns that consistently outperform the fully invested buy-and-hold strategy (red dotted line). For instance, given a $1 investment in January 1980, the strategy would generate a value of around $38 in excess of the risk-free rate, which surpasses the $15 generated by the strategy that buys and holds the market. Overall, the strategy increases Sharpe ratios over the buy-and-hold approach by around 28%. The right panel of Figure5plots the drawdown for both strategies. The main strategy's time under water is usually concentrated in recessionary periods but reverts shortly after. The buy-and-hold approach similarly incurs large market losses during recessions but it requires a long period to recoup these losses.
During recessions, past returns are positively weighted when estimating the current expected return. It is therefore important to control for the time-series momentum factor of Moskowitz, Ooi, and Pedersen (2012). Panel B of TableVshows that the alpha drops to 4.56% from 5.46% after I control for the market and the time-series momentum factor (TMom), but it is still highly statistically significant with at-statistic of 3.32. The strategy also overlaps with the volatility-managed market factor (TVol) of Moreira and Muir (2017), since both take less risk in bad times (e.g., see Figure4). Panel B shows that the alpha is equal to 4.44% (t-statistic = 3.19) when controlling for the market and TVol. Finally, controlling for TMom and TVol at the same time shows the alpha dropping by around 1.4 percentage points. Overall, these results indicate that the measure of expected returns,μ̂t+1|t$\hat{\mu }_{t+1|t}$, is related to simple strategies that rely on the positive return autocorrelation and to strategies that avoid high-volatility periods. Nevertheless, the alpha estimate of 4.0% (t-statistic = 3.55) is economically significant even after accounting for the TMom and TVol factors.
To assess the robustness of the main result, I perform an extensive battery of tests. I briefly describe them here and provide more details in theInternet Appendix.
I first document that the strategy generates similar alphas across different subsample periods and for a wide range of pricing factors. I find that the alphas are similar if I consider both conditional expected returns and time-varying volatility in the strategy's conditional beta. I also show that the strategy produces fairly large alphas under tight leverage and short-selling constraints, even if I preclude the strategy from using them and that transaction costs would need to be incredibly high to make the strategy unprofitable. I further show that the main strategy's performance is robust to the regression-based timing measures of Treynor and Mazuy (1966) and Henriksson and Merton (1981). In addition, the strategy remains profitable if I use utility-based performance measures or the manipulation-proof performance measures of Goetzmann et al. (2007). I next assess the economic relevance of the two key model ingredients (i.e.,ρμ,r<0$\rho _{{\mu ,r}}<0$andϕ(St)$\phi (S_t)$varying across economic states) by turning off each one separately and reestimating the model out of sample. I find that the profitability of the strategy disappears once I impose these constraints. Moreover, I document similar profitability for characteristic-sorted portfolios, such as value, size, and momentum. When I extend the out-of-sample analysis and start in 1960 by using lagged information about the NBER recessionary index, I find similar profitability measures for this extended sample period.
While the return predictability results in SectionIV.Aand the robustness checks discussed so far are out of sample, the sample period contains only five full business cycles. Thus, the concern remains that the results might be spurious or sample specific. To address this concern, theInternet Appendixextends the sample by looking at the cross section of U.S. state portfolios and regional business cycles. This robustness check is consistent with the main results.
Taken together, the positive estimates of unconditional alphas and the return predictability results in theInternet Appendixindicate that the econometric specification described in SectionIIprovides a good measure of expected returns over monthly horizons.
article.V. Unveiling the Unconditional Alpha:
In this section, I show that the trading strategy's alpha comes from timely identifying the business cycle state and exploiting the state-dependent autocorrelation in returns.
I start by reporting results for the hypothetical scenario that assumes perfect NBER recessionary foresight.22This exercise can be thought of as reflecting a situation in which investors could correctly foresee recessions with ex post NBER accuracy. Column (2) of TableVshows that the ex post NBER economic state leads to an increase in alphas relative to the primary strategy, producing, for example, a market alpha of 5.9%. This result is reassuring because it suggests that better estimates of business cycle turning points increase the strategy's performance.
The models under columns (3), (4), and (5) in TableVdo not condition on the state of the business cycle. In column (3), I do not allow for time-varying recessionary probabilities in the expected returns measure by fixingπt|t$\pi _{t|t}$to its unconditional value ofπss≈13%$\pi _{ss} \approx 13\%$for each periodt$t$in equation (6). Alternatively, the forecasts under column (4) only consider the predictive density associated with low variability of expected returns (i.e.,ϕ(St=Exp.)$\phi (S_t = Exp.)$in both states, mild reversals in returns, and negative forecasting weights). Finally, the forecasts under column (5) consider the predictive density associated with highϕ$\phi$(i.e., highϕ(St=Rec.)$\phi (S_t = Rec.)$in both states, momentum in returns, and positive forecasting weights).23Notably, these three alternative models generate positive alphas of around 1.4%, 0.8%, and 3.9%, respectively. However, these alphas are considerably smaller than those obtained under the main specification.
To help us understand where these alphas are coming from, Panel A of TableVIreports the out-of-sampleR2$R^2$statistic,ROOS2$R^2_{OOS}$, computed over the full sample period and theROOS2$R^2_{OOS}$obtained over expansionary or recessionary periods only.24The main specification delivers a strong predictor of excess market returns, with a monthlyROOS2$R^2_{OOS}$of around 1.7%$\%$(p$p$-values below 0.01). To further examine in which periodsμ̂t+1|t$\hat{\mu }_{t+1|t}$outperforms its benchmark, in FigureIA4in theInternet AppendixIfollow Goyal and Welch (2008) and report the cumulative squared prediction errors of the benchmark model minus the cumulative squared prediction error ofμ̂t+1|t$\hat{\mu }_{t+1|t}$. The figure shows that the slope of the cumulative difference in squared prediction errors is mostly positive over the evaluation period. This result suggests that the predictive power ofμ̂t+1|t$\hat{\mu }_{t+1|t}$is not concentrated in relatively short-lived periods.
Panel A of TableVIalso reports theROOS2$R^2_{OOS}$obtained using only NBER recessionary and expansionary periods. Consistent with the literature, most of the predictability comes from recessionary periods (ROOS,Rec.2≈4.0%$R^2_{OOS, Rec.} \approx 4.0\%$).25In contrast to this literature, the striking degree of the strategy's predictability stems predominantly from being able to also predict market returns during expansions with anROOS2$R^2_{OOS}$of around 1%$\%$.26Essentially, the main strategy allows for state-dependent time-variation in the return forecast, thereby stabilizing the forecast and preventing overfitting in those periods when expected returns do not move much.
Alternative models predict well in some states but perform poorly in others. For instance, the model in column (6) that assumes strong momentum in returns is able to predict well in recessions with monthlyROOS2$R^2_{OOS}$values around 5%$\%$, but performs poorly in expansions with negative statistics of−3%$-3\%$(a negativeROOS2$R^2_{OOS}$means that the simple recursive mean forecast produces lower forecasting errors). Conversely, the models that assume mild reversals in returns are able to predict well in expansions (ROOS,Exp.2≈0.9%$R^2_{OOS, Exp.} \approx 0.9\%$), by sacrificing the predictability in recessions (ROOS,Rec.2≈−0.8%$R^2_{OOS, Rec.} \approx -0.8\%$).
Panel B of TableVIreports the average maximum drawdown in expansions and recessions. The main takeaway is that exploiting mean reversion in returns during expansions reduces average drawdowns during these periods (e.g., see column (5)). Conversely, a strategy that accounts for strong time-series momentum while in recessions decreases average drawdowns during these times (e.g., see column (6)).
Overall, the lower average maximum drawdowns, together with the largeROOS2$R^2_{OOS}$statistics across economic states, suggest that a strong feature of the U.S. stock market is that it exhibits mild mean reversion in periods of broad economic growth but strong time-series momentum during contractions.
This section shows that the strategy's unconditional alpha comes mainly from taking a negative risk exposure at the onset of a recession and substantially increasing risk-taking shortly after.
Before discussing regressions, I present this result graphically in Figure6. I group periodt$t$excess return forecasts (using only data up until timet−1$t-1$) into five bins (low to high). For each bin, I show average values and 95% confidence intervals for four different measures. The top panel depicts results for the conditional risk exposure and the recession probabilities for montht$t$. The bottom panel shows estimates for the excess returns realized over montht$t$from a buy-and-hold strategy and the main trading strategy.
Figure6shows that when the excess return forecast is in the first bucket, average future excess returns are negative, and the probability of being in a recession is exceptionally high. The trading strategy generates a substantial part of its profits by avoiding these predictable negative returns. Note that at such times, the conditional risk exposure is negative and the trading strategy's excess return is high. The figure further shows that when the excess return forecast is in the last bucket, the recession probability is also high and future excess returns are positive and large in magnitude. The strategy benefits from these higher-than-average returns by increasing risk-taking. Notably, in buckets 2 through 4 all four measures are fairly flat.
I now turn to the statistical analysis and I estimate three monthly regressions. In each regression, the dependent variable isrte,μ$r^{e,\mu }_{t}$, the strategy's excess return in montht$t$. The regressors are a combination of (i) the market excess return,rte$r^{e}_{t}$, (ii) the NBER recessionary indicator,INBER,t$ I_{\text{NBER},t}$, and (iii) an up-market indicator,Iup-market,t$I_{\text{up-market},t}$, which equals one if the cumulative excess market return in the past six months, including timet$t$, is positive and zero otherwise.
The strategy overlaps with the volatility-managed portfolio of Moreira and Muir (2017) since both take less risk during recessions. However, one main difference between these two strategies is during recessions when to get back in. FigureIA3in theInternet Appendixshows that the volatility strategy slowly returns to the market only when volatility starts to decrease, not necessarily when the market rebounds. Therefore, volatility-timing strategies may stay out of the market too long and miss subsequent higher-than-average excess returns.27In theInternet Appendix, I conduct formal spanning tests across these portfolios and find that their returns do not span each other.
During expansions, the strategy's conditional risk exposure is related to common measures of expected returns documented in the literature. In the interest of brevity, I briefly describe the results here. I present the full estimation results in theInternet Appendix.
During expansions, when survey measures of investors' expectations about future stock market returns (e.g., Greenwood and Shleifer (2014)), investor sentiment measures (e.g., Baker and Wurgler (2006)), or flows into equity-oriented mutual funds increase, the strategy takes a contrarian position and reduces risk-taking. In good times, these results are broadly consistent with studies in the behavioral literature suggesting that many investors hold extrapolative expectations about returns.28After a period of good stock market performance, one that generates high sentiment, many investors become more bullish. Extrapolators rebalance their portfolios into equity and, in doing so, bid up current stock prices even further and reduce subsequent stock returns. Under this scenario, the strategy mildly decreases the portfolio weights on stocks. However, during recessions, this negative relation disappears, both economically and statistically.
During expansions, the strategy's profits also can be explained in terms of risk. When economic conditions deteriorate (but not enough to generate a recession) or when prices decrease more than dividends (representing an increase in risk premia), the strategy increases risk-taking. These results imply that, during expansions, the strategy has a strong countercyclical component. However, during recessions, this relation flips sign: an increase in the dividend-price ratio or a decrease in macro growth is positively related to the strategy's risk exposure.
article.VI. Economic Mechanisms:
What underlying economic mechanisms could explain my findings? I first discuss rational expectations models with countercyclical risk premia. Negative predictable returns precisely during bad times are puzzling from the perspective of these models. I then consider a model in which investors do not have rational expectations about future cash flows. My findings appear to be consistent with a sluggish adjustment in stocks to recession news. I find support for this view in analysts' cash flow forecasts.
It is difficult to rationalize a negative equity premium at the onset of bad times. Rational asset pricing models generally specify a stochastic discount factor that is a function of state variables linked to the economy, such as the surplus consumption ratio (e.g., Campbell and Cochrane (1999)), the conditional volatility of expected consumption growth (e.g., Bansal and Yaron (2004)), the time-varying probability of a large drop in consumption (e.g., Gabaix (2012), Wachter (2013), Bansal, Kiku, and Yaron (2012)), or the health of the financial sector (e.g., the intermediary-based models of Sannikov and Brunnermeier (2012), He and Krishnamurthy (2013), and Moreira and Savov (2017)). By imposing the main asset pricing equation, these state variables determine variation in the equity premium. In these models, the early stages of a recession are precisely those periods when compensation for taking risk is positive and high. Things are bad (e.g., consumption declines substantially relative to habit, real uncertainty spikes, the health of the financial sector deteriorates), and they could get worse.
In the context of these models, the unconditional alpha of the strategy would be either negative or close to zero, not positive and economically significant. As noted by Lewellen and Nagel (2006), when the conditional CAPM holds, the unconditional alpha depends on how the conditional risk exposure,βt$\beta _t$, covaries with the equity premium,γt$\gamma _t$, and with the conditional variance,σt2$\sigma ^2_t$, that is,α≈cov(βt,γt)−γσM2cov(βt,σt2)$\alpha \approx \text{cov}(\beta _t, \gamma _t ) - \frac{\gamma }{\sigma ^2_{M}} \text{cov}(\beta _t, \sigma ^2_t)$(e.g., see equation (22)). First, consider the case in whichγt$\gamma _t$andσt2$\sigma ^2_t$are not related andβt$\beta _t$covaries only withγt$\gamma _t$(i.e.,α≈cov(βt,γt)$\alpha \approx \text{cov}(\beta _t, \gamma _t)$). A strategy that sells at the onset of recessions will tend to earn a negative unconditional alpha because it avoids taking risk (i.e.,βt$\beta _t$is low) when things are scariest and compensation for risk is high (i.e.,γt$\gamma _t$is high). Now consider the case in whichγt$\gamma _t$andσt2$\sigma ^2_t$are positively related, as many asset pricing models imply.29In this case, the effect of time-varying volatility offsets the risk premium impact and shrinks the negativeα$\alpha$toward zero. Hence, for each model, the net effect on alpha will depend on the two offsetting channels' relative strength. Consistent with this view, Moreira and Muir (2017) simulate data from leading equilibrium asset pricing models and show that the volatility-managed alpha, that is, the alpha from a strategy that reducesβt$\beta _t$whenσt2$\sigma ^2_t$is high, is either centered at zero (e.g., for long-run risk and intermediary-based models) or shows a weak negative deviation from zero alpha (e.g., for habits and time-varying rare-disasters models).
Overall, under rational expectations, most models would suggest that the types of price movements common at the onset of recessions would be associated with higher, not lower, discount rate forecasts.
These price dynamics imply that overestimating future cash flows at the onset of recessions under the subjective measure imputes negative predictability of returns under the econometrician's one. If these mistakes are undone rather quickly, returns can be quite negative in the short run and mean-revert shortly thereafter. To see this, Figure7plots the impulse response of the log price-dividend ratio(∂pdt+k∂εtg)$(\frac{\partial pd_{t+k}}{\partial \varepsilon _{t}^{g}})$and the log one-period return(∂rt+k∂εtg)$(\frac{\partial r_{t+k}}{\partial \varepsilon _{t}^{g}})$to a one-standard-deviation surprise decrease in the expected dividend growth series. For the impulse responses, I takeκ1=0.969$\kappa _1 = 0.969$,γ1=0.3541/12$\gamma _1 = 0.354^{1/12}$, andσg=0.058/12$\sigma _g = 0.058/\sqrt {12}$from table II in van Binsbergen and Koijen (2010) and useλ=0.17$\lambda = 0.17$from table III in Bouchaud et al. (2019). A negative shock to expected dividend growth immediately decreases the price-dividend ratio (left panel) and generates a negative return on stocks (right panel). Because subjective beliefs about lower cash flows react to this shock with a lag, expected dividends are too high relative to the rational benchmark. Hence, the price-dividend ratio and the realized return fall, but they do not fall enough (see the red dots). In the subsequent periods, agents sluggishly adjust their dividend growth forecast downward to the rational level. In doing so, the price-dividend ratio slowly converges to the rational level, and returns on stocks continue to be negative. After four months, the shock is fully impounded into prices and the price dynamics under subjective and rational expectations move in sync.
Under this subjective belief framework, my one-month-ahead return forecastμ̂t+1|t$\hat{\mu }_{t+1|t}$equalsμt+λB2(1−κ1λγ1)∑j=0∞(λγ1)jεt−jg$\mu _{t} + \lambda B_{2} (1- \kappa _1\lambda \gamma _{1})\sum _{j=0}^{\infty } (\lambda \gamma _{1} )^{j} \varepsilon _{t-j}^{g}$. It therefore incorporates two components. The first is the rational risk premia component,μt$\mu _t$, that negatively affects price-dividend ratios (e.g., see the present-value restriction in (37)). The second,λB2(1−κ1λγ1)∑j=0∞(λγ1)jεt−jg$\lambda B_{2} (1- \kappa _1\lambda \gamma _{1})\sum _{j=0}^{\infty } (\lambda \gamma _{1} )^{j} \varepsilon _{t-j}^{g}$, captures near-term forecastable changes in cash flows that are not yet incorporated into prices. Rather than isolating these two components,μ̂t+1|t$\hat{\mu }_{t+1|t}$gives the sum of the two, which is the relevant object from a one-period market-timing perspective.
For long-term investors, the composition of expected returns might be relevant as cash flow shocks die out quicker than discount rate shocks since the latter seem to be more persistent. Consistent with this view, Moreira and Muir (2019) find that determining the composition of shocks is important for setting the investment horizon in portfolio choice. Empirically, it is possible to disentangle these two expected return components. For instance, one can start with the state-space model of van Binsbergen and Koijen (2010) described by equation (29) and impose the present-value restrictions under subjective expectations as in De La O and Myers (2021). By imposing sticky expectations, one gets the measurement equation for returns in (38). One could then use the diffusion index extracted in SectionII.Cto determine the economic regime in place and to sharpen inference about expected dividend growth and its shocksεtg$\varepsilon _{t}^{g}$. These shocks, in turn, enter the measurement equation for returns. Finally, one can infer the parameterλ$\lambda$using forecast errors and forecast revisions as in Bouchaud et al. (2019). I leave these extensions of the model to future work.
A model of subjective beliefs, where belief updating is sluggish relative to the econometrician, can explain the negative return predictability at the onset of recessions. Next, I provide suggestive evidence in favor of this explanation. Specifically, I show that downward revisions in analyst expectations about cash flows are exceptionally high, and forecasts are too optimistic when the equity premium forecast is negative.
To compute forecast errors and revisions, I proceed as follows.32First, I construct cash flow subjective expectations using analyst-by-analyst earnings per share (EPS) forecasts from the Thomson Reuters unadjusted I/B/E/S Detail History File. I use analyst EPS forecasts rather than dividends per share forecasts because the former series starts in 1976, while the latter begins in 2003. I focus on the forecast made for the quarterly figures reported in the current and the next fiscal quarter (i.e., Forecast Period Indicator (FPI) variable equal to 6 or 7), and construct the median forecast using 30-day buckets around the quarterly fiscal period. To do so, for each firm I calculate the median of all forecasts submitted during each 30-day bucket. If an analyst makes more than one forecast for the same firm, the same fiscal quarter, and the same 30-day bucket, I consider only the last observation to ensure that I have the most up-to-date forecast. Next, I match these median forecasts with the actual EPS observation using the I/B/E/S unadjusted actuals file database. Consistent with prior studies, I use the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP) cumulative adjustment factors to account for stock splits that transpired during the period between the EPS forecast and the earnings announcement. Finally, I define forecast errors as the difference between the actual EPS observation for fiscal quartert$t$and the corresponding median earnings forecast. Likewise, I define forecast revisions as the change in the median earnings forecast for fiscal quartert$t$between two consecutive 30-day buckets. I further normalize both of these differences by the stock price before the previous fiscal quarter announcement.
article.VII. Conclusion:
I use data on a variety of economic indicators to identify business cycle turning points in real time. Using these estimates, I document a strong feature of the U.S. stock market: time-series reversals in periods of broad economic growth but time-series momentum during contractions. I then propose a new measure of expected returns that utilizes the state-dependent autocorrelation in returns. This trading strategy earns large alphas, increases Sharpe ratios, and generates big utility gains for a mean-variance investor.
The positive alpha of the strategy is puzzling from the perspective of standard models with rational expectations and countercyclical risk premia. The strategy takes less risk at the onset of bad economic times, showing that returns are predictably negative, not high, during these periods. After cashing out, my strategy returns to the market during up-markets in recessions. This result suggests that the average investor is late in acting on the macroeconomic information that signals the onset of recessions. Finally, I provide suggestive evidence in favor of this explanation by looking at analyst forecasts.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
